summary:
The Hunnic Invasions of Europe (c. 370–455 AD) began when the Huns, a Turkic or Mongolic nomadic people from the Eurasian steppe, crossed the Volga River around 370 AD and rapidly overwhelmed the Ostrogothic and Visigothic confederacies north of the Black Sea. The initial Hunnic advance drove hundreds of thousands of Goths across the Danube into the Roman Empire as refugees, setting in motion the chain of events that led to the catastrophic Roman defeat at the [[event:2793|Battle of Adrianople]] (378 AD). The Huns themselves remained north of the Danube for decades, extracting tribute from both Rome and the Germanic peoples they dominated.

Under [[event:2035|Attila the Hun]] (r. 434–453 AD), who united the Hunnic tribal confederation into a formidable empire centered in the Hungarian plain, the invasions reached their peak intensity. Attila systematically ravaged the Balkans (destroying dozens of cities), extorted enormous tribute payments from Constantinople, invaded Gaul in 451 (halted at the [[event:2036|Battle of the Catalaunian Plains]]), and invaded Italy in 452. The sudden disintegration of the Hunnic Empire after Attila's death in 453 AD — as subject Germanic peoples revolted and defeated the Huns at the Battle of Nedao (454 AD) — demonstrated how dependent the nomadic empire was on a single leader. The Huns quickly faded as a political force, but their role in triggering the [[event:2038|Germanic Migration Period]] permanently reshaped the map of Europe.
